food_subgroup: Baked rice dish with curry sauce, fish otah, and melted cheese topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean baked rice with Nyonya otah and Nanyang curry. FOODON_00002572 (baked rice food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Singaporean baked rice with Peranakan curry and otah.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Singaporean baked rice dish. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a full baking dish of curry rice topped with Nyonya otah and melted cheese, as typically served at Singaporean café-style restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from HPB Singapore Foodo: Nasi Lemak with Curry (rice component), USDA FoodData Central: Curry sauce (coconut-based), and peer-reviewed composition data for Nyonya otah (grilled spiced fish cake)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for baked rice with Nanyang curry and Nyonya otah. Nutrient values are estimated by component: steamed white rice (~55% by weight, HPB Foodo), Nanyang coconut curry sauce (~25% by weight, USDA coconut-based curry), handmade Nyonya otah (~15% by weight, peer-reviewed SEA fish cake data), and melted cheese topping (~5% by weight, USDA cheddar). Weighted composite per 100 g calculated accordingly. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-based mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on white rice with coconut curry sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked rice with Nanyang curry and Nyonya otah. GI estimated at 58 based on the analogous food 'white rice with coconut-based curry sauce' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. The fat and protein content from cheese and otah moderately attenuate the GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 47.4, rounded to 47.7 using refined carbohydrate estimate of 82.2 g per 380 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g; ~1,482 mg per serving) from curry sauce, otah seasoning, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load per serving, which may approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-sauce or reduced-cheese preparation may help.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~47.7) driven by a large portion of white rice with medium GI (58) and moderate carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the large white rice base produces a high glycemic load per serving; portion reduction, substitution with brown rice if available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g) from coconut milk curry and cheese, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of coconut-milk-based curry and melted cheese contributes meaningful saturated fat and sodium per serving, which warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk; occasional consumption within a heart-healthy dietary pattern is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Rice with Nanyang Curry and Handmade Nyonya Otah (BR7) is a Singaporean café-style fusion dish that combines two beloved local culinary traditions: the Peranakan Nyonya kitchen and the Nanyang (Southeast Asian Chinese) spice palette. The dish is prepared using a duo-cooking process: steamed white rice is first cooked and then arranged in a baking dish, topped with a rich, aromatic Nanyang coconut curry sauce and handmade Nyonya otah — a traditional Peranakan grilled spiced fish cake made from blended fish paste, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, and chilli, typically wrapped and steamed or grilled in banana leaf. The assembled dish is then blanketed with a generous layer of cheese and baked in an oven until the cheese melts to a golden, bubbling crust. The result is a dish with contrasting textures: soft, curry-infused rice beneath a crispy, savoury cheese crust, with the fragrant, slightly spicy otah providing a distinctly Peranakan flavour note. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~178 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein from fish and cheese, significant saturated fat from coconut milk and dairy, and a high glycemic load per serving due to the white rice base. The sodium content is elevated, making it a dish of concern for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. It is typically served as a main course at Singaporean café-style restaurants and is associated with a waiting time due to the baking step. The dish reflects Singapore's multicultural food heritage, blending Peranakan, Chinese, and Malay culinary influences into a single comfort-food format.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any approved primary source for this specific composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using HPB Foodo (rice), USDA FoodData Central (coconut curry, cheese), and peer-reviewed Southeast Asian food composition data (Nyonya otah / fish cake). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be treated as moderate-confidence approximations pending direct laboratory analysis or an exact HPB Foodo entry. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimation. 3. Selenium is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from component fatty acid profiles and should be treated as approximations. 5. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on white rice with coconut curry sauce; the cheese and fish protein content moderately attenuate the GI relative to plain white rice. gi_category is 'medium' per the Veritas threshold rule (56–69 inclusive). 6. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Flavours by Saute, Funan); formal halal certification has not been independently verified. 7. The dish is served at one restaurant (Flavours by Saute, Funan, Singapore) as of the field survey data collection date. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (58 × 82.2 g carbohydrate per 380 g serving) / 100 ≈ 47.7.
